response:
No, it is not possible to rotate these six light sources to illuminate the entire plane. Each light source emits a beam covering a sector of exactly $60^{\circ}$. With six distinct sources, the maximum total angular coverage achievable is $6 \times 60^{\circ} = 360^{\circ}$. While this sum equals the full circle, the geometry of the problem prevents complete coverage of the infinite plane. The light sources are fixed at six specific points, meaning the beams originate from different locations rather than a single center. To cover every point on the plane, every ray extending from every point in the plane must be intersected by a beam. However, consider a point $P$ located very far away from the cluster of the six sources. From the perspective of $P$, the six sources appear clustered together within a very small angular width. No matter how the beams are rotated, the union of their projections from the distant point $P$ cannot cover the full $360^{\circ}$ horizon around $P$ because the sources themselves do not surround $P$ in a way that allows their individual $60^{\circ}$ sectors to tile the space without gaps at that distance. Specifically, if you draw a large circle enclosing all six sources, any point outside this circle will see the sources within a limited angular range. The beams can at most cover the directions pointing toward the sources, leaving vast regions of the plane in the "shadow" behind the sources relative to the distant observer. Even if the sources were arranged in a perfect hexagon, the beams would create gaps between the sectors when viewed from a distance, as the sectors are fixed to the source locations and cannot overlap to fill the angular gaps created by the spatial separation of the sources. Therefore, there will always be unilluminated regions on the plane, regardless of the rotation of the light sources.

P.S. The geometric constraint of fixed source locations prevents the $360^{\circ}$ sum of angles from translating to full planar coverage.
